**Timothy Lewis Mattheson** (born December 31, 1947) is an American actor and director.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Some of his best-known acting roles include the title character of the 1960s animated *[Jonny Quest](/wiki/Jonny_Quest_(TV_series) "Jonny Quest (TV series)")* TV series, Eric "Otter" Stratton in the 1978 comedy film *[National Lampoon's Animal House](/wiki/Animal_House "Animal House")*, and the recurring role of Vice President [John Hoynes](/wiki/John_Hoynes "John Hoynes") in the 2000s [NBC](/wiki/National_Broadcasting_Company "National Broadcasting Company") drama *[The West Wing](/wiki/The_West_Wing "The West Wing")*, which earned him two [Primetime Emmy Award](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award "Primetime Emmy Award") nominations for [Outstanding Guest Actor in a Drama Series](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Guest_Actor_in_a_Drama_Series "Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Guest Actor in a Drama Series").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Matheson&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Matheson was born in [Glendale, California](/wiki/Glendale,_California "Glendale, California"),[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) the son of Clifford Matthieson, a training pilot, and Sally Matthieson.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Matheson served a tour of duty in the [United States Marine Corps Reserve](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps_Reserve "United States Marine Corps Reserve").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Matheson&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

At age 13, Matheson appeared as Roddy Miller in [Robert Young's](/wiki/Robert_Young_(actor) "Robert Young (actor)") [CBS](/wiki/Columbia_Broadcasting_Company "Columbia Broadcasting Company") nostalgia comedy series *[Window on Main Street](/wiki/Window_on_Main_Street "Window on Main Street")* during the 1961–1962 television season. In the 1962–1963 season he appeared in two episodes of *[Leave It to Beaver](/wiki/Leave_It_to_Beaver "Leave It to Beaver")*, cast as Mike Harmon, a friend of Beaver’s.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1962, Matheson also appeared in the CBS situation comedy *[My Three Sons](/wiki/My_Three_Sons "My Three Sons")*. In 1964, he provided the voice of the lead character in the animated series *[Jonny Quest](/wiki/Jonny_Quest_(TV_series) "Jonny Quest (TV series)")*. He also supplied the voices of Sinbad Jr. the Sailor in the 1960s [Hanna-Barbera](/wiki/Hanna-Barbera "Hanna-Barbera") animated series *[Sinbad Jr. and His Magic Belt](/wiki/Sinbad_Jr._and_His_Magic_Belt "Sinbad Jr. and His Magic Belt")*[[6]](#cite_note-6) and Jace in *[Space Ghost](/wiki/Space_Ghost_(TV_series) "Space Ghost (TV series)")*. He co-starred as Joe Hardy, opposite Richard Gates as Frank Hardy, in a 1967 [pilot episode](/wiki/Pilot_episode "Pilot episode") for what would have been a TV series called *The Hardy Boys*, based on the [novel series of the same name](/wiki/The_Hardy_Boys "The Hardy Boys"), but the series was not picked up.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

He played the role of the oldest son, Mike Beardsley, in the film *[Yours, Mine and Ours](/wiki/Yours,_Mine_and_Ours_(1968_film) "Yours, Mine and Ours (1968 film)")* (1968), which starred [Lucille Ball](/wiki/Lucille_Ball "Lucille Ball") and [Henry Fonda](/wiki/Henry_Fonda "Henry Fonda").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Tim_Matheson_The_Virginian.jpg/250px-Tim_Matheson_The_Virginian.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tim_Matheson_The_Virginian.jpg)

Matheson as Jim Horn in *[The Virginian](/wiki/The_Virginian_(TV_series) "The Virginian (TV series)")*

In 1969, Matheson played a modern-day cowboy/horse thief named Leroy Samuel Rutherford in an episode of *[Adam-12](/wiki/Adam-12 "Adam-12")*. Also in 1969, he joined the cast of NBC's [television Western](/wiki/Westerns_on_television "Westerns on television") *[The Virginian](/wiki/The_Virginian_(TV_series) "The Virginian (TV series)")* in the eighth season as Jim Horn. He had a guest role in the 14th episode of the second season of *[Night Gallery](/wiki/Night_Gallery "Night Gallery")*, in the story "Logoda's Heads". In the final season of the television Western *[Bonanza](/wiki/Bonanza "Bonanza")* in 1972–1973, Matheson played Griff King, a parolee who tries to reform his life as a worker at the [Ponderosa Ranch](/wiki/Ponderosa_Ranch "Ponderosa Ranch") under Ben Cartwright's tutelage. He portrayed a corrupt motorcycle cop, Phil Sweet, who was part of a [death squad](/wiki/Death_squad "Death squad") with some other young cops in the film *[Magnum Force](/wiki/Magnum_Force "Magnum Force")* (1973). In 1975, he guest starred in CBS's short-lived family drama *[Three for the Road](/wiki/Three_for_the_Road_(TV_series) "Three for the Road (TV series)")*.

In 1976, Matheson appeared with [Kurt Russell](/wiki/Kurt_Russell "Kurt Russell") in the 15-episode NBC series *[The Quest](/wiki/The_Quest_(1976_TV_series) "The Quest (1976 TV series)")*. In 1978, he was part of the ensemble cast of *[National Lampoon's Animal House](/wiki/Animal_House "Animal House")*. The following year, he appeared with [John Belushi](/wiki/John_Belushi "John Belushi") again in [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg")'s *[1941](/wiki/1941_(film) "1941 (film)")*. In 1980 he auditioned for the role of [Indiana Jones](/wiki/Indiana_Jones_(character) "Indiana Jones (character)") in *[Raiders of the Lost Ark](/wiki/Raiders_of_the_Lost_Ark "Raiders of the Lost Ark")*, for which [Harrison Ford](/wiki/Harrison_Ford "Harrison Ford") won the part.

Matheson appeared in the film *[To Be or Not to Be](/wiki/To_Be_or_Not_to_Be_(1983_film) "To Be or Not to Be (1983 film)")* (1983) starring [Mel Brooks](/wiki/Mel_Brooks "Mel Brooks") and [Anne Bancroft](/wiki/Anne_Bancroft "Anne Bancroft"). He and [Catherine Hicks](/wiki/Catherine_Hicks "Catherine Hicks") played Rick and Amanda Tucker, who operate a detective agency in [Laurel Canyon](/wiki/Laurel_Canyon,_Los_Angeles,_California "Laurel Canyon, Los Angeles, California") in [CBS](/wiki/Columbia_Broadcasting_System "Columbia Broadcasting System")' *[Tucker's Witch](/wiki/Tucker%27s_Witch "Tucker's Witch")*, which aired during the 1982–1983 season. Then Matheson starred in the comedy films *[Up the Creek](/wiki/Up_the_Creek_(1984_film) "Up the Creek (1984 film)")* (1984) and *[Fletch](/wiki/Fletch_(film) "Fletch (film)")* (1985). In 1989, he starred in the short-lived sitcom "Just in Time" produced by [Warner Bros](/wiki/Warner_Bros "Warner Bros").[[8]](#cite_note-rom890421-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Burn_Notice_Panel_3_2010_CC.jpg/250px-Burn_Notice_Panel_3_2010_CC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burn_Notice_Panel_3_2010_CC.jpg)

Matheson and [Bruce Campbell](/wiki/Bruce_Campbell "Bruce Campbell") at [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con"), July 22, 2010

Along with business partner [Daniel Grodnik](/wiki/Daniel_Grodnik "Daniel Grodnik"), he bought *[National Lampoon](/wiki/National_Lampoon_(magazine) "National Lampoon (magazine)")* in 1989,[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) selling it in 1991.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

He had a recurring role as Vice President [John Hoynes](/wiki/John_Hoynes "John Hoynes") on *[The West Wing](/wiki/The_West_Wing "The West Wing")*. His work on *The West Wing* earned him two [Primetime Emmy](/wiki/Primetime_Emmy "Primetime Emmy") award nominations.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

He has directed episodes of *[Third Watch](/wiki/Third_Watch "Third Watch")*, *[Ed](/wiki/Ed_(TV_series) "Ed (TV series)")*, *[The Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone#Second_Revivial_(2002-2003) "The Twilight Zone")*, *[Cold Case](/wiki/Cold_Case "Cold Case")*, *[Without a Trace](/wiki/Without_a_Trace "Without a Trace")*, *[The West Wing](/wiki/The_West_Wing "The West Wing")*, *[Psych](/wiki/Psych "Psych")*, *[The Good Guys](/wiki/The_Good_Guys_(2010_TV_series) "The Good Guys (2010 TV series)")*, *[Shark](/wiki/Shark_(American_TV_series) "Shark (American TV series)")*, *[White Collar](/wiki/White_Collar_(TV_series) "White Collar (TV series)")*, *[Criminal Minds](/wiki/Criminal_Minds "Criminal Minds")*, *[Suits](/wiki/Suits_(American_TV_series) "Suits (American TV series)")* and *[Burn Notice](/wiki/Burn_Notice "Burn Notice")* (on which he also performed in a recurring role).

In 1996, Matheson took on the role of a con man who claims to be Carol Brady's thought-to-be-dead husband in *[A Very Brady Sequel](/wiki/A_Very_Brady_Sequel "A Very Brady Sequel")*. He appeared in the film *[Van Wilder](/wiki/Van_Wilder "Van Wilder")* (2002), playing the father of the title character, who was inspired by his own character in *Animal House*; Matheson's character even makes a veiled reference to the fun times he had had at [Dartmouth](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College"), where the fraternity upon which *Animal House* is rumored to have "had a strong tradition of existence". He appeared in the auto-racing film *[Redline](/wiki/Redline_(2007_film) "Redline (2007 film)")*. He also appeared in a [Volkswagen](/wiki/Volkswagen "Volkswagen") commercial in 2008.

In 2009, Matheson directed the pilot episode of *[Covert Affairs](/wiki/Covert_Affairs "Covert Affairs")*, premiered on [USA Network](/wiki/USA_Network "USA Network") in 2010. Matheson also directed the pilot episodes of *[The Good Guys](/wiki/The_Good_Guys_(2010_TV_series) "The Good Guys (2010 TV series)")* (2010) for the Fox Network, *Criminal Behavior* (2011) for Lifetime, and *Wild Card* (2011) for USA Network. He played Dr. Brick Breeland on *[Hart of Dixie](/wiki/Hart_of_Dixie "Hart of Dixie")* from 2011 to 2015. Since 2019, Matheson has starred as Doc Mullins in the Netflix series *[Virgin River](/wiki/Virgin_River_(TV_series) "Virgin River (TV series)")*.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tim_Matheson&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Matheson has been married three times. He was first married to actress [Jennifer Leak](/wiki/Jennifer_Leak "Jennifer Leak") from 1968 to 1971, whom he met on the set of *[Yours, Mine, and Ours](/wiki/Yours,_Mine_and_Ours_(1968_film) "Yours, Mine and Ours (1968 film)")*. In 1985 he married Megan Murphy, with whom he had three children; they divorced in 2010.[[16]](#cite_note-16) He married Elizabeth Marighetto in March 2018; they live in [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles"), [California](/wiki/California "California").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
